overview.body: MASTER unit. Module 3 ends here. You've already built file save/load, parallel lists for destinations, tracking lists for the route and hazards, and a library full of reusable helpers. This unit pulls it all together. You'll replace the inline hazard if/elif with three more parallel lists, sort the hazards alphabetically for display, count unique cities visited using the in operator, and generate a polished trip report saved to trip_report.txt that the player can keep as a souvenir. The function_library.py grows by exactly one function this unit: get_random_hazard(). Everything else lives in lab-3-3.py.

mentorQuote.quote: MASTER unit is about the deliverable. You've spent two units building infrastructure — files, lists, library functions, save/load. This unit the player gets something they can hold onto. A printed report. A permanent record. Treat trip_report.txt the way you'd treat any shipping artifact: the format is fixed, the content is real, and it has to look right every time.

objectives: Add three more parallel lists for hazards: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS (6 hazards each, same shape as the destination lists)
Add get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs) to function_library.py (parameterized: accepts the hazard lists as arguments)
Refactor the in-game hazard block to use the library helper instead of an inline if/elif
Sort hazards alphabetically in the trip summary using .copy() and .sort()
Count unique cities visited using the in operator
Build save_trip_report() in lab-3-3.py and write a formatted multi-section report to trip_report.txt at the end of every game
overview.keyConceptsText: Parallel lists applied to hazards (same pattern as destinations, fresh data). .copy() to duplicate a list before mutating. .sort() for alphabetical ordering in place. in operator for membership testing and deduplication. Formatted multi-section file output. Library Membership Rule: a function with known future use originates in function_library.py from day one. get_random_hazard() will be used again in Module 4 when hazard data moves to a text file, so it lands in the library now.
overview.textbookReference: Chapter 7: Lists and Tuples (Sections 7.4 'The in operator', 7.5.4 '.sort()', 7.5.6 '.copy()', 7.7 'Files and lists')
programRequirements: Your program MUST use three parallel lists for hazards: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS
Your program MUST use .copy() followed by .sort() to display hazards alphabetically (the original hazards_list stays in arrival order for the save file)
Your program MUST build a unique_cities list using the in operator
Your program MUST write trip_report.txt at the end of every completed game (no y/n prompt — always save)
Your save_trip_report() function MUST live in lab-3-3.py, not in the library (it's lab-specific format)
overview.filenameNote: Save your main game file as lab-3-3.py. Your function_library.py grows by exactly one function this unit. The save file savegame.txt and the trip report trip_report.txt are generated at runtime; you do not submit them.

dataFiles.format: Your reusable function module. Carries forward from Lab 3.2 with one addition this unit: get_random_hazard() in a new HAZARD HELPER section.

namedConstants.name: All previous constants (distance, MILES_MULTIPLIER, HAZARD_CHANCE, parallel destination lists)
REPORT_FILE
namedConstants.description: NEW — where to write the trip report ('trip_report.txt')

namedConstants.name: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS
namedConstants.description: NEW — three parallel lists, six hazards each

Add the parameterized helper to function_library.py

Build unique_cities with the in operator

beforeYouBegin.intro: MASTER unit pulls Module 3 together. The library grows by one function (get_random_hazard); the lab file gets three new parallel lists (hazards), one new helper (save_trip_report), and a few additions to the trip summary (sorted hazards, unique city count, file output).
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- IN function_library.py ----
flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage()     (keep from 2.2)
get_valid_int(), get_yes_no()                   (keep from 2.3)
save_game(...), load_game()                     (keep from 3.2)
display_destinations(...), get_destination(...), get_redirect(...)  (keep from 3.2)
display_numbered_list(...)                      (keep from 3.2)
get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs)   [NEW-CP3]

# ---- IN lab-3-3.py ----

# ---- DISTANCE + PARALLEL DESTINATION CONSTANTS ----   (keep from 3.2)
SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, ... HAZARD_CHANCE
SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, SA_NOTES
HOU_DESTINATIONS, HOU_DISTANCES, HOU_NOTES
AUS_DESTINATIONS, AUS_DISTANCES, AUS_NOTES

# ---- NEW HAZARD CONSTANTS ----
REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt'                  [NEW-CP7]
HAZARD_NAMES = [...]      (6 hazards)            [NEW-CP2]
HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS = [...] (6 messages)           [NEW-CP2]
HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS = [...] (6 messages)           [NEW-CP2]

def get_city_data(city):                         (keep from 3.2)

def save_trip_report(filename, ...):             [NEW-CP7]
│── Writes formatted multi-section report to disk
│── Stays in lab-3-3.py (lab-specific format, not library-bound)

# ---- MAIN MENU LOOP ----   (keep from 3.2)
while in_program:
│── New / Load / Quit
│
│── # ---- PLAY-AGAIN LOOP ----   (keep from 3.2)
│   while play_again:
│   │── Reset per-game state
│   │
│   │── # ---- IN-GAME LOOP ----   (keep from 3.2)
│   │   while playing:
│   │   │── Menu, choice, gate flip (unchanged)
│   │   │── Travel loop with hazards
│   │   │   on hazard: hazard, heads_msg, tails_msg =
│   │   │              function_library.get_random_hazard(
│   │   │                  HAZARD_NAMES,
│   │   │                  HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS,
│   │   │                  HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS)          [REPLACED-CP4]
│   │   │── Standard arrival / save / quit handling
│   │
│   │── After in-game loop — FINAL TRIP REPORT (on screen):
│   │   ├── status = 'BEACH WINNER!' / 'Trip Incomplete' / etc.
│   │   ├── sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy(); .sort()  [NEW-CP5]
│   │   ├── unique_cities = build with `in` operator         [NEW-CP6]
│   │   ├── Display all the sections on screen
│   │   └── save_trip_report(REPORT_FILE, ...)               [NEW-CP7]
│   │
│   │── Update session accumulators, play_again prompt
│
│── After play-again: SESSION STATISTICS (keep from 3.2)

content.body: A clean starting point for Lab 3.3. Copy lab-3-2.py to lab-3-3.py, update the docstring, and confirm everything still runs before adding the MASTER-unit features.
Same reason as every BUILD/ENHANCE cleanup: do not mix 'fix the old' with 'add the new.' If something breaks this unit, it's because of something you added today, not something that broke yesterday.

items: Copy lab-3-2.py to lab-3-3.py in the same folder
Do NOT copy function_library.py — it stays in place; you'll add one function to it in CP3
Update the docstring: change the title to Lab 3.3: Trip Report, update the description, and change the chapter list
Run lab-3-3.py as-is. Play one game to the beach. Verify the trip summary appears with YOUR ROUTE and HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED. If anything's different from Lab 3.2, fix the copy before moving on

content.body: No new BookEx patterns yet — this is pure cleanup. CP2 starts the new work.
Run lab-3-3.py end-to-end. Play one full game. Verify behavior is identical to Lab 3.2. Choose 3 to quit when done.

rows: Editing lab-3-2.py directly
Always preserve your previous submission. Copy first, then edit the copy.
Touching function_library.py this CP
Library changes start in CP3. This CP only renames a file and updates a docstring.

items: A file named lab-3-3.py with an updated docstring
function_library.py unchanged from Lab 3.2
Running lab-3-3.py plays a complete game identical to Lab 3.2

content.quote: Last unit you saw what parallel lists do for destinations. This unit, you do the same trick for hazards — but with three lists per item instead of two. The name, the escape message, and the delay message. Same pattern, fresh data. And the payoff scales: when you want a seventh hazard, you add one item to each list and the rest of your code doesn't change. I love this pattern. It's the moment your code stops feeling like a wall of if statements and starts feeling like a data table.

content.body: Three parallel lists in lab-3-3.py: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, and HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS. Each list has six items, and items at the same index describe the same hazard. The current hazard if/elif chain in the in-game loop stays in place for now — you'll refactor it in CP4 after the library helper is built.

Same idea as the destination lists from Lab 3.2: same index across three lists = related data. With six hazards instead of three, the savings are bigger. The current inline if/elif handles 3 hazards in ~12 lines. Six hazards via parallel lists is 18 strings of data and zero lines of branching logic. That ratio gets better as the hazard count grows. Module 4 takes this to its conclusion by moving the data out of code entirely — into a text file.

items: Find a spot in lab-3-3.py after the destination parallel lists and before def get_city_data. Add a section header: # HAZARD DATA AS PARALLEL LISTS (NEW in 3.3)
Declare HAZARD_NAMES as a list with six strings: "Buc-ee's appears!", "Armadillo crossing!", "Speed trap!", "Check engine light!", "Whataburger cravings!", "Toll road confusion!"

Declare HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS as a list with six matching messages (one per hazard, in the same order). These print when the escape coin flip is HEADS — the player escapes. Examples: "You resist the beaver's call.", "It scurries across safely.", "You saw the cop in time.", "False alarm. Reset the light.", "You drive past. Strong willpower.", "Exact change. Smooth pass."

Declare HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS as a list with six matching messages. These print on TAILS — the player gets delayed. Examples: "Lost 2 hours on beef jerky.", "You swerved. Armadillo unbothered.", "Small town officer says howdy.", "Pulled over. Mechanic shrug.", "Detour through the drive-thru.", "Wrong lane. Backed up half a mile."
Do NOT remove the existing if/elif hazard block yet. The lists exist but nothing uses them until CP4

content.body: Program 7-1 (list_demo.py) and Section 7.2.2 (a list with initial values) — the same pattern from Lab 3.2, applied to a different kind of data.
Add a temporary debug print right after the list declarations to verify all three lists are length 6 and the index ordering is consistent:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
print(f'DEBUG: HAZARD_NAMES len      = {len(HAZARD_NAMES)}')
print(f'DEBUG: HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS len = {len(HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS)}')
print(f'DEBUG: HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS len = {len(HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS)}')
print(f'DEBUG: All match length 6?   = '
      f'{len(HAZARD_NAMES) == len(HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS) == len(HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS) == 6}')
for i in range(len(HAZARD_NAMES)):
    print(f'  [{i}] {HAZARD_NAMES[i]} | H: {HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS[i]} | T: {HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS[i]}')

content.body: Run the program. The DEBUG block should print all six hazards with their matching HEADS and TAILS messages, and the lengths-match line should read True. If the messages don't match the hazards (e.g., Armadillo paired with a Buc-ee's message), reorder the lists until each row makes sense. Delete the DEBUG block before moving to CP3.

rows: Lists are different lengths
Every parallel list MUST have the same length (6). If they don't match, indexing into one will eventually crash. Use the DEBUG check to verify.
Wrong order across lists
Read the lists vertically by index, not horizontally. HAZARD_NAMES[2] is 'Speed trap!' — so HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS[2] should be the escape message for a speed trap, not for an armadillo.
Escaping the apostrophe in "Buc-ee's"
Use double quotes around any string that contains an apostrophe: "Buc-ee's appears!". Or escape it: 'Buc-ee\\'s appears!'. Double quotes are cleaner.
Forgetting commas
Each list item must end with a comma except the last. Missing commas cause silent string concatenation: ['a' 'b'] becomes ['ab'] with no error.

items: Three new list declarations at module level: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS
All three lists are length 6 in the same order (index 0 = Buc-ee's, index 1 = Armadillo, ...)
DEBUG check confirms lengths match and the rows make sense
Game still plays identically to Lab 3.2 (the lists exist but nothing uses them yet)

content.body: One new function in function_library.py: get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs). It accepts three parallel lists as parameters and returns a random triple. The function lives in the library — not in lab-3-3.py — because Module 4 will reuse it when hazard data moves to a text file.

This is the Library Membership Rule in action: a function with a known future use goes in the library from day one. We're not going to write get_random_hazard in lab-3-3.py and then promote it next unit — we learned that lesson with save_game/load_game in Lab 3.2. Going forward, when you can see the future, put the function where it belongs the first time.

items: Open function_library.py. Find the DESTINATION HELPERS section
After the destination helpers (right after get_redirect ends), add a new section header: # HAZARD HELPER (Added in Lab 3-3)
Define get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs) using the skeleton below
Inside the function: use random.randint(0, len(names) - 1) to get a valid index, then return the three list items at that index as a tuple
Save function_library.py. You'll wire it up in CP4

content.code:
def get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs):
    """
    Pick a random hazard from three parallel lists.

    Args:
        names (list of str): Hazard names
        heads_msgs (list of str): Escape-success messages (same index)
        tails_msgs (list of str): Escape-fail messages (same index)

    Returns:
        tuple: (name, heads_msg, tails_msg) at a random index
    """
    # 1. Pick a random index in range [0, len(names) - 1]:
    #    index = random.randint(0, len(names) - 1)
    # 2. Return the three values at that index as a tuple:
    #    return names[index], heads_msgs[index], tails_msgs[index]
    pass

content.body: Section 5.6: returning multiple values via tuples. Section 7.3: list indexing. The function does in 2 lines what a 12-line if/elif chain does.
Step 1: Test the function in isolation. At the bottom of function_library.py, find the existing if __name__ == '__main__': block. Add a temporary call:

content.code:
# Add temporarily to function_library.py's main() for testing:
test_names = ['hazard1', 'hazard2']
test_heads = ['escape1', 'escape2']
test_tails = ['delay1', 'delay2']
result = get_random_hazard(test_names, test_heads, test_tails)
print(f'  get_random_hazard test => {result}')

content.body: Step 2: Run function_library.py directly: python3 function_library.py. You should see one of ('hazard1', 'escape1', 'delay1') or ('hazard2', 'escape2', 'delay2'). Run it a few times — both options should appear. Delete the test block after you're confident.

rows: NameError: name 'random' is not defined
function_library.py already imports random at the top — if you got this error, you may have deleted the import. Restore it.
IndexError when calling the function
random.randint(0, len(names) - 1) — that's len() - 1, not len(). Indexing is 0-based so the last valid index is one less than the length.
Function returns a single value instead of a tuple
Make sure the return statement is return names[index], heads_msgs[index], tails_msgs[index] (three values separated by commas). Parentheses optional but commas required.
Reading from HAZARD_NAMES globals
function_library.py doesn't know about your lab's globals. The function MUST use the parameter names (names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs), not HAZARD_NAMES.

items: get_random_hazard() defined in function_library.py
Function uses parameters (no module-level globals)
Function returns a 3-tuple
Isolated test confirms it returns different values across runs

content.body: A refactor of the hazard logic in the in-game loop. Out: the inline if hazard_type == 1: ... elif hazard_type == 2: chain. In: a single call to function_library.get_random_hazard(HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS) that returns the hazard name, the heads message, and the tails message.

The if/elif chain works, but it only handles three hazards. To add a fourth, you'd need another elif branch — and a wider random.randint range, and matching hazard/heads_msg/tails_msg assignments. The parallel-list version handles six hazards with the same line count as it took to handle three. Adding a seventh is one item per list.

items: Find the hazard block in main()'s in-game loop — the part inside if function_library.check_percentage(HAZARD_CHANCE):. There's a random.randint(1, 3) followed by an if/elif/else chain that sets hazard, heads_msg, and tails_msg
Delete the random.randint(1, 3) and the entire if/elif/else block. Keep the print(), print('*** HAZARD! ***'), and the hazards_faced increment that come before it
Replace them with one call: hazard, heads_msg, tails_msg = function_library.get_random_hazard(HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS)
Keep the rest unchanged — the print(hazard), the hazards_list.append(hazard), the escape coin flip, and the print(heads_msg)/print(tails_msg) branches all stay as-is

content.body: Section 5.6 (returning multiple values) — the unpacking pattern a, b, c = func() is the same shape you used for get_destination in Lab 3.2.

Step 1: Run lab-3-3.py. Start a New Game. Play through long enough to trigger at least one hazard. Verify the hazard name shows up (could be any of the six now, not just the original three from Lab 3.2).

Step 2: Play a few more games. You should eventually see hazards 4-6 (Check engine, Whataburger, Toll road) appear — the new additions. If you only ever see the first three, your get_random_hazard is using random.randint(0, 2) instead of random.randint(0, len(names) - 1).

Step 3: When a hazard fires, verify the HEADS and TAILS messages match the hazard. Buc-ee's should mention beavers/jerky, not armadillos. If messages are mismatched, the lists are out of order — revisit CP2.

rows: NameError: 'hazard_type' is not defined
You deleted the random.randint but left a reference to hazard_type somewhere below. Search for hazard_type in the file.
Tuple unpacking error
The function returns three values. Your call site must unpack all three: hazard, heads_msg, tails_msg = function_library.get_random_hazard(...). Three names on the left, comma-separated.
Hazard messages don't match the hazard
The lists are out of order. Look at the DEBUG output from CP2 to verify.
Same hazard every time
Your call site is using the same index for all three lookups (e.g. HAZARD_NAMES[0] hardcoded). Use the library function — let it pick the random index once.

items: The hazard block in main() has no inline if/elif for hazard selection
The block calls function_library.get_random_hazard(HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS)
All six hazards can appear in gameplay (verified over multiple runs)
HEADS/TAILS messages match the hazard

checkpoints.title: Sort Hazards Alphabetically in the Trip Summary

content.quote: The player just survived a road trip. Their brain is still in the chaos of it. The trip summary is where we give them order. A sorted list of hazards reads differently than a chronological one — alphabetical feels like a record, like an index, like finalized. Two lines of code, big UX win.

content.body: A sorted copy of hazards_list that gets displayed alphabetically in the trip summary. The original hazards_list stays in arrival order — save_game needs the chronological version, and the player's trip narrative matches arrival order too. So we copy first, sort the copy, display the copy. The original is untouched.

list.sort() is a destructive operation — it changes the list in place. If you call hazards_list.sort() directly, you've permanently lost the arrival order. .copy() creates an independent duplicate; sort the duplicate and the original stays intact. This is the same .copy() pattern you saw in get_redirect in Lab 3.2 — preserve the source, mutate the copy.

items: Find the trip summary block in main() — the part that prints YOUR ROUTE and HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED. It comes after the in-game loop ends and before the play-again prompt
Immediately before the HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED display, add two lines: sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy() and sorted_hazards.sort()
Change the display call from displaying hazards_list to displaying sorted_hazards
Update the section title from 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED' to 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z)' so the sort order is visible to the player
Important: only the display uses sorted_hazards. The save_game call (if it fires) still passes the original hazards_list in arrival order. Don't touch that path

content.body: Section 7.5.4 (list.sort()): sorts a list in place alphabetically by default. Section 7.5.6 (list.copy()): creates an independent copy so the source list is preserved. Together they're the standard 'sort-without-mutating' pattern in Python.

Step 1: Play a game where you trigger multiple hazards. Hazard names start with a mix of letters (A, B, C, S, T, W). Verify the trip summary shows them alphabetically — e.g., 'Armadillo crossing!' before 'Buc-ee's appears!' even if Buc-ee's happened first.
Step 2: Verify the section title now reads HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z). The underline should adjust automatically because display_numbered_list uses '-' * len(title).

Step 3 (save-roundtrip check): Play a game with multiple hazards, save mid-trip, quit, reload. The saved hazards in savegame.txt should be in arrival order (NOT sorted), because save_game still receives the original list. If your save file is alphabetical, you accidentally sorted hazards_list in place.

rows: hazards_list.sort() instead of sorted_hazards.sort()
Sorts the original list in place, losing arrival order. The save file will then save sorted hazards. ALWAYS copy first.
hazards_list = hazards_list.sort()
Common trap: .sort() returns None. This line replaces your list with None. Use the two-line pattern instead.
Trip summary uses hazards_list instead of sorted_hazards
You created sorted_hazards but kept passing the old name to display_numbered_list. Double-check the variable name in the display call.
Title doesn't update automatically
The divider line in display_numbered_list uses '-' * len(title). If you hardcoded 19 dashes for 'HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED', they won't grow when you add ' (A-Z)'.

items: sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy() followed by sorted_hazards.sort() right before the display call
Trip summary shows hazards alphabetically with section title HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z)
Save file still contains hazards in arrival order

content.quote: visited_cities is the full route in arrival order, including every time the player came back through San Antonio. If they visit Austin and bounce back to SA before heading to Houston, the list has San Antonio twice. That's correct for the route display — the player wants to see they went back — but the unique cities count shouldn't double-count. Build the deduplicated list with the in operator. And then test it. Go to Austin and come back. If the count says you visited five cities instead of four, you have a bug.

content.body: A second list, unique_cities, built from visited_cities by filtering out duplicates with the in operator. The count of unique cities goes in the trip summary right after the route block, and the full list goes into the trip report file in CP7.

The in operator is the cleanest way to check whether something already exists in a list. The pattern is: empty list, loop through the source, append to the new list only if the item isn't there yet. Four lines. This is what Python's set() type does internally — you'll learn that in Module 4. For now, the explicit pattern is the learning.

items: In main()'s trip summary block, right after the sorted_hazards lines from CP5 (or just before, doesn't matter), add the unique-cities pattern
Initialize an empty list: unique_cities = []
Loop through visited_cities: for city in visited_cities:
Inside the loop, check membership: if city not in unique_cities: then unique_cities.append(city)
Display the count right after the route display: print(f'Unique cities visited: {len(unique_cities)}') and then print()

content.body: Section 7.4 (the in operator): tests whether an item exists in a list. Program 7-9 (search_demo.py): building a filtered list with not in. Section 7.5.5 (len()): returns the count of items in a list.
Step 1 (the simple case): Play SA → Houston → Galveston. Trip summary should show:

content.output:
YOUR ROUTE
----------
  1. San Antonio
  2. Houston
  3. Galveston

Unique cities visited: 3

content.body: Three cities visited, three unique. No duplicates to filter. Easy.
Step 2 (the Austin round-trip): Play SA → Austin → back to SA → Houston → Galveston. Trip summary should show:

content.output:
YOUR ROUTE
----------
  1. San Antonio
  2. Austin
  3. San Antonio
  4. Houston
  5. Galveston

Unique cities visited: 4

content.body: Five cities in the route, four unique. If your unique count says 5, your in check isn't working. If it says 3, you're filtering San Antonio entirely instead of just deduplicating it.

rows: Unique count equals route length
Your not in check isn't preventing the duplicate append. Walk through the loop manually: when city = 'San Antonio' for the second time, what does 'San Antonio' in unique_cities return?
Unique count is 0 or off by some other amount
You initialized unique_cities inside the loop instead of before it, so the list gets reset every iteration. Move the unique_cities = [] line to BEFORE the loop.
SyntaxError on if city not in unique_cities:
Watch the wording: it's not in (two words), not !in or !=in. Python reads not in as a single operator.
Order matters for unique count
unique_cities ends up in first-appearance order (San Antonio first, Austin second, ...). That's correct for the count; if you ever need a sorted unique list, copy and sort it (same pattern as CP5).

items: unique_cities built in the trip summary block using the in operator
print(f'Unique cities visited: {len(unique_cities)}') displayed after the route
The Austin round-trip test gives count = 4 (not 5)

checkpoints.title: Build save_trip_report() and Generate the Report

content.quote: This is the deliverable. Every other lab this module was about machinery — can you write to a file, can you build a list, can you organize data. This unit we hand the player something. A multi-section report with their name on it, their route, their statistics. It needs to look right every time, save automatically at the end of every game, and reflect what just happened. No y/n prompt — we don't ask. The report is part of the game.

content.body: Two things: (1) a new save_trip_report function in lab-3-3.py that writes a formatted multi-section report to trip_report.txt, and (2) a call to it at the end of every game (immediately after the on-screen trip summary, before the play-again prompt). The function stays in lab-3-3.py — it's lab-specific format, not a general-purpose helper.

Library functions are universal mechanics. save_trip_report is the opposite — every section header, every label, every layout choice is specific to this game. The Library Membership Rule doesn't promote it. It lives next to the game logic that needs it. This is the other half of the rule: not everything goes in the library.

items: At module level in lab-3-3.py, define a new constant near the other constants: REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt'

Define save_trip_report as shown in the skeleton. It takes 11 parameters in this order: filename, player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, visited_cities, unique_cities, sorted_hazards, status

Inside the function, open the file in 'w' mode and write the five sections in order: HEADER, ROUTE, STATISTICS, HAZARDS, closing divider. Use ' -> '.join(visited_cities) for the arrow-separated route string

The status string — you'll need to set this in main() based on game outcome: 'BEACH WINNER!' if they reached the beach, 'Quit without saving' if they chose option 3 mid-trip, or 'Trip Incomplete' otherwise
In main(), in the trip-summary block, set the status variable before displaying anything. Place it right where the existing print(f'Status: ...') logic is (or add that print if it's not there)

Call save_trip_report after all the on-screen displays but BEFORE the play-again prompt. Pass all 11 arguments including REPORT_FILE, unique_cities, and sorted_hazards that you computed in CP5 and CP6
Print a confirmation: print(f'Trip report saved to {REPORT_FILE}.') followed by print()
No y/n prompt. The report saves every game, automatically

content.code:
def save_trip_report(filename, player_name, current_city,
                     total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards,
                     total_escaped, visited_cities, unique_cities,
                     sorted_hazards, status):
    """
    Write a formatted multi-section trip report to disk.

    Stays in lab-3-3.py (not in function_library.py) because the
    report format is specific to this game.
    """
    # 1. open(filename, 'w') as outfile (or use outfile = open(...))
    # 2. Write the header block:
    #    - 'DEEP IN THE HEART - TRIP REPORT'
    #    - '=' * 40
    #    - f'Driver: {player_name}'
    #    - f'Final City: {current_city}'
    #    - f'Status: {status}'
    #    - blank line
    # 3. Write the ROUTE block:
    #    - 'ROUTE:'
    #    - f'  {city1 -> city2 -> ...}'  (use ' -> '.join(visited_cities))
    #    - f'  Unique cities visited: {len(unique_cities)}'
    #    - blank line
    # 4. Write the STATISTICS block:
    #    - 'STATISTICS:'
    #    - f'  Total Miles: {total_miles}'
    #    - f'  Total Turns: {total_turns}'
    #    - f'  Hazards Faced: {total_hazards}'
    #    - f'  Hazards Escaped: {total_escaped}'
    #    - if total_hazards > 0:
    #        rate = (total_escaped / total_hazards) * 100
    #        f'  Escape Rate: {rate:.1f}%'
    #    - blank line
    # 5. Write the HAZARDS (A-Z) block:
    #    - 'HAZARDS (A-Z):'
    #    - if sorted_hazards is non-empty: loop and write f'  - {hazard}'
    #    - else: write '  None - Lucky traveler!'
    #    - blank line
    # 6. Write the closing divider: '=' * 40
    # 7. Close the file
    pass

content.body: Section 6.1 (writing to files): open(filename, 'w'), .write(), .close(). Section 7.7 (writing lists to files): looping through a list and writing each item. Section 7.5.7 (str.join()): combine list items with a separator string between them — ' -> '.join(['a', 'b', 'c']) gives 'a -> b -> c'.
Step 1: Play one full game to the beach. After the on-screen trip summary, you should see Trip report saved to trip_report.txt.
Step 2: Open trip_report.txt in your editor. The content should look like this:

content.output:
DEEP IN THE HEART - TRIP REPORT
========================================
Driver: TestPlayer
Final City: Galveston
Status: BEACH WINNER!

ROUTE:
  San Antonio -> Houston -> Galveston
  Unique cities visited: 3

STATISTICS:
  Total Miles: 450
  Total Turns: 3
  Hazards Faced: 1
  Hazards Escaped: 1
  Escape Rate: 100.0%

HAZARDS (A-Z):
  - Armadillo crossing!

========================================

content.body: Step 3: Play a game that ends in 'Trip Incomplete' (decline to return from Austin, or decline to continue after Houston). Verify the Status: line in the report changes to Status: Trip Incomplete.
Step 4: Play a game with NO hazards (short trip to Austin and back). Verify the HAZARDS section reads None - Lucky traveler! instead of an empty bulleted list.
Step 5: Play TWO games in a row. Verify trip_report.txt contains the SECOND game's data — the file is overwritten each game, not appended to. (That's what 'w' mode does.)

rows: trip_report.txt not created
Most likely: save_trip_report is defined but never called. Confirm there's a call in main() right before the play-again prompt.
File created but empty
You opened the file but didn't write anything. Add print statements inside save_trip_report to confirm the function actually runs.
File appends instead of replacing
You used 'a' mode instead of 'w' mode. Mode 'w' overwrites; 'a' appends to the end. Use 'w'.
TypeError: 'list' object is not callable
Probably wrote visited_cities() or sorted_hazards() with parentheses somewhere. These are lists, not functions — no parentheses.
Escape rate divides by zero crash
You wrote rate = (total_escaped / total_hazards) * 100 without the if total_hazards > 0: guard. Wrap the line in a conditional.
Report has the previous game's hazard count
You passed hazards_faced (the per-leg counter that gets reset) instead of total_hazards (the per-game total). Walk the variable names.

items: save_trip_report defined in lab-3-3.py (NOT in function_library.py)
REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt' constant at module level
main() sets status based on outcome and calls save_trip_report after the on-screen summary
Confirmation message Trip report saved to trip_report.txt. appears in the terminal
trip_report.txt exists after every completed game
Report has all five sections in order: HEADER, ROUTE, STATISTICS, HAZARDS, closing divider

beforeYouSubmit: function_library.py grew by exactly one function this unit: get_random_hazard(names, heads_msgs, tails_msgs) in a new HAZARD HELPER section
lab-3-3.py has the three parallel hazard lists at module level: HAZARD_NAMES, HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS, HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS (all length 6)
Hazard block in the in-game loop uses function_library.get_random_hazard(...) — no inline if/elif for hazard selection
sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy() followed by sorted_hazards.sort() in the trip summary
save_trip_report called automatically at the end of every game (no y/n prompt)
trip_report.txt contains all five sections: HEADER, ROUTE, STATISTICS, HAZARDS, closing divider
Both files have updated docstrings with name, abc123, section, and description
All Module 3 features still work: three-loop architecture, save/load, parallel destinations, tracking lists, session statistics
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–7 only. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions, function_library, file I/O, try-except, lists, append, index, len, in)
.copy() — create an independent duplicate of a list
.sort() — sort a list in place alphabetically
.join() — combine list items into a single string with a separator
Member of (or not member of) a list: x in list, x not in list
Multi-return tuples from a function and unpacking at the call site
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Dictionaries, sets, list comprehensions, readlines(), with statement, sorted() (the built-in function that returns a new list — use .sort() on a copy instead), or any Chapter 8+ techniques.

finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Module 4 starts on top of this. function_library.py grows again next unit when data starts loading from text files.

finalCheck.expectedReportNote: After the canonical run completes, trip_report.txt should contain exactly this content. All five sections present, in this order, with these labels and dashes. Different gameplay produces different numbers, but the shape must match byte-for-byte.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Hazards still show only the original three (Buc-ee's, Armadillo, Speed trap)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 — the hazard block is still using the inline if/elif. Delete the entire if hazard_type == 1: ... elif ... block and replace with the single library call.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Hazard fires but the HEADS or TAILS message is wrong for that hazard
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 — the parallel lists are out of order. HAZARD_NAMES[2] must match HAZARD_HEADS_MSGS[2] and HAZARD_TAILS_MSGS[2]. Re-run the DEBUG check from CP2 to verify.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip summary shows hazards in arrival order, not alphabetical
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 — either you skipped the .copy()+.sort() step, or you're still passing hazards_list to the display call instead of sorted_hazards.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Save file (savegame.txt) shows hazards alphabetically too
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 — you sorted hazards_list in place (hazards_list.sort()) instead of sorting a copy. The save needs arrival order; only the display gets sorted.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Unique city count equals total cities (e.g., 5 instead of 4 in the Austin round-trip)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6 — the if city not in unique_cities check isn't blocking the duplicate append. Walk through the loop on paper for the Austin scenario.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: trip_report.txt doesn't exist after a completed game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7 — save_trip_report is defined but never called from main(). Confirm there's a call AFTER the on-screen summary and BEFORE the play-again prompt.
Check CP7 — the function probably opens the file but never writes to it. Verify each outfile.write(...) line has an actual string argument.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: trip_report.txt contains old game's data after a new game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7 — you used file mode 'a' (append) instead of 'w' (write/overwrite). Use 'w'.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: ZeroDivisionError when computing escape rate on a no-hazard game
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7 — the escape rate calculation needs an if total_hazards > 0: guard. With zero hazards, dividing by zero crashes.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Status line shows None or is missing
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP7 — the status variable isn't being set in all code paths. Make sure it's defined for BEACH WINNER, Trip Incomplete, AND Quit without saving outcomes.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 7-9 (the in operator), 7-13 (writing a list to a file), and 7-16 (sorting a list) — these three cover every new technique this unit

Verify the parallel hazard lists FIRST before doing anything else. If CP2 is off by one across the three lists, every CP after it will produce weird output. The DEBUG check from CP2 catches this in 10 seconds
When testing CP6's unique-city counter, use the SA → Austin → SA → Houston → Galveston path explicitly. It's the only path that meaningfully exercises the dedup logic with the current city graph

For CP7, open trip_report.txt in Notepad after every test run. Visually compare against the canonical expected report in the final check. Tiny formatting differences (extra newline, missing colon) WILL be flagged by CodeGrade
Submit to CodeGrade early and often. function_library.py in particular is grader-checked in isolation — a wrong signature in get_random_hazard shows up immediately
Come to Zoom or office hours if anything's unclear. This is the final lab of Module 3; the patterns here all reappear in Module 4 with different data
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 7 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every Chapter 7 example. Sections 7.4, 7.5.4, 7.5.6, 7.5.7, and 7.7 are the relevant ones for this lab.
